claim: Chain of thought prompting (encouraging models to think step-by-step with explicit intermediate steps) improves LLM performance across tasks by making reasoning visible and debuggable
actionable: When building prompts, explicitly instruct the model to break tasks into numbered steps and not skip any, showing detailed intermediate reasoning before producing the final output
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Few-shot prompting (providing in-context examples) significantly outperforms zero-shot prompting for task-specific classification and alignment, especially when industry-specific definitions differ from general ones
actionable: For subjective classification tasks, include 2-5 representative labeled examples in the prompt to align model outputs with your domain-specific definitions before asking for new classifications
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Prompt chaining (decomposing multi-step tasks into separate prompts with intermediate outputs) enables better debugging, component-by-component optimization, and easier identification of performance bottlenecks vs a single monolithic prompt
actionable: Break complex multi-step workflows into 2-4 separate chained prompts where each intermediate output is human-reviewable, then separately optimize each prompt based on which component is weakest
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Fine-tuning on recent/narrow data often overfits and causes models to lose general-purpose capabilities; modern approach is to avoid fine-tuning and instead use prompt engineering, RAG, or agentic workflows which allow easy upgrades when new base models release
actionable: Avoid fine-tuning when possible; instead use prompt engineering, few-shot examples in prompts, RAG, or tool-augmented agentic workflows so you can immediately swap in better base models without retraining
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Prompt templates with variable interpolation (inserting user metadata like role, language preference, location) allow personalization at scale without building separate prompts for each user or persona
actionable: Build parameterized prompt templates where role, language, skill level, or other user attributes are inserted as variables; update these templates dynamically based on user context rather than maintaining separate prompts
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) solves hallucination, outdated knowledge, and lack of sourcing problems by embedding documents into vector databases and retrieving relevant context before generation, enabling grounded responses without model retraining
actionable: For knowledge-dependent tasks (medical, legal, current events), implement RAG by embedding your documents into a vector database, retrieving top matches for user queries, and appending them to the prompt with a sourcing instruction
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Chunking (storing both full document and chapter/section-level embeddings in vector DB) and HyDE (generating hypothetical document to match user query intent better) improve RAG relevance for large documents where vanilla embedding similarity fails
actionable: For large documents (pharma docs, medical manuals), store hierarchical embeddings (full + chapter level); use HyDE by generating a hypothetical answer to the user query and embedding that for retrieval instead of the raw query
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Agentic AI workflows are multi-step processes combining prompts, tools (APIs/databases), memory, and intermediate reasoning loops; they differ from single-prompt RAG by enabling back-and-forth user interaction, tool use, and autonomous planning
actionable: Design agent systems as: (1) user query → (2) agent planning/reasoning → (3) tool/API calls → (4) context update → (5) user response; treat the agent like a manager delegating tasks, not a single inference step
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Working memory vs archival memory separation in agents improves latency: frequently accessed data (user name) sits in fast working memory while infrequent data (birthday) goes to slower archival memory to avoid expensive lookups on every interaction
actionable: In your agent architecture, separate user context into hot (working) memory for frequently-used data and cold (archival) memory for rare data; retrieve working memory once per conversation turn to avoid repeated expensive lookups
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standardized agent-to-tool communication layer that allows agents to discover tool requirements and negotiate parameters dynamically, scaling better than hardcoded API integrations
actionable: For multi-tool agent systems, use MCP servers instead of hardcoding API documentation; MCPs allow agents to ask tools 'what do you need?' and discover requirements dynamically for better composability
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: LLM judges with rubrics (defining scoring criteria like 'a 5 is when summary is under 100 chars and mentions 3+ key points') are effective for automating evals of subjective agent outputs at scale, beating manual human grading
actionable: Create evaluation rubrics for subjective outputs (politeness, clarity, relevance) and use an LLM judge with few-shot examples to automatically grade agent responses across test cases, replacing manual evaluation
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Component-based evaluation (testing each step separately: extraction, lookup, drafting, sending) plus end-to-end metrics (user satisfaction) together identify bottlenecks better than end-to-end alone, enabling targeted optimization
actionable: Design evals with both component-level tests (does step 2 correctly format data?) and end-to-end tests (user satisfaction); use component failures to guide optimization effort rather than only measuring final output quality
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Objective vs subjective eval distinction: objective evals (e.g., order ID matches user input) can be checked with code, while subjective evals (politeness, relevance) need LLM judges or human review; separate these to guide debugging
actionable: In your eval suite, clearly separate checkable objective failures (data mismatches, API errors) from subjective assessments (tone, helpfulness); automate objective checks and use LLM judges for subjective ones
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Multi-agent systems are useful when you have parallelizable independent tasks or when agents can be reused across teams; flat topologies work for equal peers, hierarchical for user-facing orchestration through a single entry point
actionable: Design multi-agent systems hierarchically (coordinator agent at top) if users interact with one interface, or as flat networks if agents are independent tools; use MCPs for agent-to-agent communication instead of direct API coupling
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Paradigm shift in agentic AI engineering: move from deterministic (structured data pipelines) to fuzzy (free-form text/images requiring dynamic interpretation); fuzzy engineering requires specialized mindset and guardrails to prevent hallucinations and attacks
actionable: When building agents, segment your workflow: use deterministic rules/code where you can, add human-in-the-loop appeals/corrections for fuzzy components, and implement explicit guardrails (output validation, rate limiting) around unstructured reasoning
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: LLM traces (logging of all prompts, intermediate outputs, tool calls in a request) are essential for debugging agentic systems; without traces, you cannot identify which step/prompt is causing failures in multi-step workflows
actionable: Implement full LLM request tracing (log all prompts, outputs, tool calls, latencies) in your agent system; use trace data to debug failures and identify the weakest component (e.g., outline generation vs email drafting)
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Real-world enterprise workflows (credit memos) show 20-60% time savings when augmenting human teams with agentic AI (agent prepares initial draft, humans review/refine) rather than agents working fully autonomously or humans working alone
actionable: Position agentic AI as productivity multipliers for human teams (analyst + agent), not replacements; focus evals on human+AI cycle time and quality vs pure automation
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Prompt engineering skill matters: study showed BCG consultants with AI + prompting training significantly outperformed those with AI alone, suggesting structured prompt design training drives measurable productivity gains
actionable: Invest time in learning and practicing prompt engineering (few-shot, chaining, role-play prompts); the techniques are learnable and drive measurable improvements in LLM task performance
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: The lecture separates centaur vs cyborg interaction patterns: centaurs delegate full tasks to AI then review; cyborgs work tightly in back-and-forth loops with AI; choice depends on task (enterprise automation favors centaur, real-time assistance favors cyborg)
actionable: For batch enterprise workflows, use centaur pattern (write detailed prompt, let AI work, review final output); for interactive tasks, use cyborg pattern (rapid back-and-forth with model, iterative refinement)
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Task decomposition with human domain experts (shadowing workflows for 1-2 days, asking where they struggle/spend time) is the right first step before building agentic workflows; this guides which steps are fuzzy vs deterministic vs needing tools
actionable: Before building any agentic system, spend 1-2 days with domain experts (support reps, analysts) documenting: their exact steps, which steps take longest, which are error-prone, which are repetitive; use this to design your agent decomposition
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Chaining improves performance by allowing intermediate review and separate debugging; even if a single monolithic prompt with step-by-step reasoning is theoretically possible, chaining's transparency and componentization make it preferable in practice
actionable: Prefer chained multi-prompt workflows over single complex prompts with step-by-step instructions; trade minor latency for significant gains in debuggability, component optimization, and maintainability
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG

claim: Fine-tuning on Slack messages (to make model 'speak like your team') often backfires by making the model act like employees (deferring, casual) rather than following instructions; this demonstrates why fine-tuning on narrow company data is risky
actionable: Avoid fine-tuning on internal communications or narrow domain data as it often learns undesired conversational patterns (informality, deference) instead of task competence; stick to prompt engineering and examples instead
source_title: Stanford CS230 | Autumn 2025 | Lecture 8: Agents, Prompts, and RAG
